text: Hello everyone, so today we will be talking about robot assisted Duhamel's procedure for Hirschsprung's disease which we did recently. I was assisted by Dr. Vipul Bothra and Dr. Mohammad Takroni from Egypt who is here as a fellow in minimal access surgery with us. It was a three year old girl child, was an already diagnosed case of Hirschsprung's disease and we had already done a diversion colostomy. The port positioning is like this with the camera just below the stoma and the two working ports in right iliac fossa and left hypochondrium and the assistant port is in the epigastric region. This is the position of the patient, slightly head low and left up to take the small bowel away. Lithotomy position so that you have access to the anal opening once you have pulled out the bowel. We preferred a side docking and these port position allow us a good panoramic view of the entire left side of the abdomen including the descending colon, sigmoid and the pelvis.

The surgeon takes levelling biopsies from the rectosigmoid junction, mid sigmoid level, and upper sigmoid near the junction with the descending colon. The biopsy sites are sutured with threads for marking. The sigmoid colon is hitched to the abdominal wall at the rectosigmoid junction and mid sigmoid level to stabilize it during mesentery dissection.

The surgeon uses Vicryl or PDS sutures transabdominally, with the camera positioned below the stoma, providing a clear view from the pelvis to the descending colon. The left port is in the left hypochondrium, the right port in the right iliac fossa, and the assistant port in the epigastrium. The surgeon begins dissection across the mesentery of the rectum, entering the mesorectal space and carrying the dissection downwards in the retro rectal region towards the anal verge, while carefully avoiding the ureters and ovaries.

The surgeon gently dissects the retrorectal fibers until reaching the anal verge, then transitions to dissecting the sigmoid mesentery. Care is taken to avoid injuring major vessels such as the superior rectal artery and branches of the inferior mesenteric artery by staying close to the mesenteric border of the colon. The dissection progresses to the mid biopsy site, guided by biopsy markers, and continues proximally to the third biopsy site after receiving the frozen section report indicating normal architecture at the proximal site.

The surgeon maintains orientation during colonic dissection by identifying hitching stitches at landmarks such as the mid sigmoid and splenic flexure. The mesentery is closely dissected, and the area in the pelvis is thoroughly washed to manage any oozing. The surgeon assesses the mobilization of the mesentery to ensure adequate length for bowel pull-down, with the option to continue dissection under pneumoperitoneum if needed.

The surgeon removes the first hitching stitch to mobilize the bowel, then applies a laparoscopic stapler at the peritoneal reflection to cleanly cut the rectal stump without spillage of intestinal contents. The rectal stump is left at the peritoneal reflection, reaching the pelvic brim, while the proximal colon is freed for retro-rectal pull-down. Concurrently, another team member prepares the anal opening for retro-rectal space entry, using hitching stitches to facilitate bowel extraction.

Long artery forceps are used to feed threads and pull the bowel through a large opening in the posterior wall of the rectum, proximal to the dentate line. The surgeon increases the CO₂ insufflation flow rate to prevent abdominal collapse due to air leakage. The descending colon is pulled out, and linear staplers are applied trans-anally to create a pouch with the anterior wall as the native rectum and the posterior wall as the pull-down colon. The abdomen remains distended with CO₂, and a thorough wash is given to the pelvis before placing a drain.
